Please note that any changes targeting the original code should remove the line number, colon, and leading space. 1: # Writing Hermes Agent Skills 2: 3: > A comprehensive guide for AI agents and human developers building, installing, and managing skills for the Hermes AI assistant (v0.16.0+). 4: 5: --- 6: 7: ## Table of Contents 8: 9: 1. [What Are Skills?](#what-are-skills) 10: 2. [Skill vs Tool — When to Use Each](#skill-vs-tool) 11: 3. [SKILL.md Format](#skillmd-format) 12: 4. [Directory Structure](#directory-structure) 13: 5. [Skill Location & Discovery](#skill-location--discovery) 14: 6. [CLI Commands](#cli-commands) 15: 7. [Hermes v0.16.0 Architecture](#hermes-v0160-architecture) 16: 8. [Configuration Deep-Dive](#configuration-deep-dive) 17: 9. [Memory System](#memory-system) 18: 10. [Gotchas & Lessons Learned](#gotchas--lessons-learned) 19: 11. [Complete Example: A "Git Commit Helper" Skill](#complete-example) 20: 12. [Quick Reference](#quick-reference) 21: 22: --- 23: 24: ## 1. What Are Skills? 25: 26: Skills are **instruction documents** that extend Hermes's capabilities without writing custom Python code. Think of them as "playbooks" — they tell the agent *how* to accomplish a task using its existing tools (shell commands, file I/O, browser, etc.). 27: 28: A skill is activated when Hermes detects that a user's request matches the skill's domain. The skill's instructions are then injected into the agent's context, guiding its behavior. 29: 30: **Key properties:** 31: - Skills are **declarative** — they describe *what* to do, not *how* to implement it 32: - Skills use **existing tools** — shell commands, file operations, built-in tools 33: - `bash 730: wc -c ~/.hermes/MEMORY.md 731: # Should be < 2200 732: 733: wc -c ~/.hermes/USER.md 734: # Should be < 1375 735: ``` 736: 737: --- 738: 739: ## 11. Complete Example: A "Git Commit Helper" Skill 740: 741: Here's a complete, production-ready skill that you can install and use: 742: 743: ### Directory Structure 744: 745: ``` 746: git-commit-helper/ 747: ├── SKILL.md 748: ├── scripts/ 749: │ └── check-staged.sh 750: └── templates/ 751: └── commit-types.md 752: ``` 753: 754: ### SKILL.md 755: 756: ```markdown 757: --- 758: name: git-commit-helper 759: description: > 760: Guides the agent through writing conventional commits with proper 761: scope, type, and breaking change annotations. Enforces team conventions 762: and validates commit messages before execution. 763: version: 1.2.0 764: author: Joel Thomas 765: license: MIT 766: platforms: 767: - linux 768: - macos 769: - windows 770: metadata: 771: hermes: 772: tags: 773: - git 774: - workflow 775: - conventions 776: related_skills: 777: - git-branch-manager 778: - code-review-checklist 779: requires_tools: 780: - shell 781: - file_read 782: --- 783: 784: # Git Commit Helper 785: 786: ## When This Skill Activates 787: - User asks to commit changes 788: - User mentions "conventional commit" 789: - User says "commit this" or "save my changes" 790: 791: ## Pre-Commit Checklist 792: Before committing, always: 793: 1. Run `git status` to check the working tree 794: 2. Run `git diff --staged --stat` to review staged changes 795: 3. If nothing is staged, ask the user what to stage 796: 4. Run any project linters if available (`npm run lint`, `ruff check .`) 797: 798: ## Commit Message Format 799: ``` 800: (): The above content does NOT show the entire file contents. Please note that any changes targeting the original code should remove the line number, colon, and leading space. 1: # LLM Model Routing Architecture 2: 3: > A comprehensive guide to building intelligent, cost-effective model routing systems that direct requests to the right LLM based on complexity, privacy, and cost constraints. 4: 5: --- 6: 7: ## Table of Contents 8: 9: 1. [Why Model Routing?](#why-model-routing) 10: 2. [The 3-Layer Pattern](#the-3-layer-pattern) 11: 3. [Layer 1: Rule-Based Router (0ms)](#layer-1-rule-based-router) 12: 4. [Layer 2: Semantic Router (5-20ms)](#layer-2-semantic-router) 13: 5. [Layer 3: LLM Classifier (100-300ms)](#layer-3-llm-classifier) 14: 6. [Privacy Override System](#privacy-override-system) 15: 7. [LiteLLM Proxy Configuration](#litellm-proxy-configuration) 16: 8. [semantic-router Library](#semantic-router-library) 17: 9. [RouteLLM Integration](#routellm-integration) 18: 10. [Logging & Observability](#logging--observability) 19: 11. [Config Pattern: Layered YAML](#config-pattern-layered-yaml) 20: 12. [Cost Analysis](#cost-analysis) 21: 13. [Gotchas & Lessons Learned](#gotchas--lessons-learned) 22: 14. [Quick Reference](#quick-reference) 23: 24: --- 25: 26: ## 1. Why Model Routing? 27: 28: Not every request needs GPT-4o or Claude Sonnet 4. A "what time is it?" question doesn't need a $0.015/1K-token model — a $0.0001/1K-token model works fine. 29: 30: **Model routing** automatically classifies incoming requests and routes them to the most cost-effective model that can handle the task. 31: 32: ### The Economics 33: 34: | Request Type | % of Traffic | Best Model | Cost/1K tokens | 35: |-------------|-------------|--- _length INTEGER NOT NULL, 744: 745: -- Routing result 746: layer TEXT NOT NULL, -- "rules", "semantic", "llm" 747: model_tier TEXT NOT NULL, -- "simple", "coding", "reasoning", "creative" 748: confidence REAL NOT NULL, 749: reason TEXT, 750: privacy_override BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE, 751: 752: -- Performance 753: routing_latency_ms REAL NOT NULL, -- Time to classify 754: model_latency_ms REAL, -- Time for model response 755: 756: -- Cost 757: input_tokens INTEGER, 758: output_tokens INTEGER, 759: cost_usd REAL, 760: 761: -- Quality 762: user_feedback TEXT -- "good", "bad", NULL 763: ); 764: 765: -- Indexes for common queries 766: CREATE INDEX idx_timestamp ON routing_decisions(timestamp); 767: CREATE INDEX idx_model_tier ON routing_decisions(model_tier); 768: CREATE INDEX idx_layer ON routing_decisions(layer); 769: ``` 770: 771: ### Useful Queries 772: 773: ```sql 774: -- Distribution by tier (last 24 hours) 775: SELECT 776: model_tier, 777: COUNT(*) as count, 778: ROUND(COUNT(*) * 100.0 / SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER(), 1) as pct 779: FROM routing_decisions 780: WHERE timestamp > datetime('now', '-1 day') 781: GROUP BY model_tier 782: ORDER BY count DESC; 783: 784: -- Average latency by layer 785: SELECT 786: layer, 787: ROUND(AVG(routing_latency_ms), 1) as avg_routing_ms, 788: ROUND(AVG(model_latency_ms), 1) as avg_model_ms 789: FROM routing_decisions 790: GROUP BY layer; 791: 792: -- Daily cost tracking 793: SELECT 794: DATE(timestamp) as date, 795: SUM(cost_usd) as daily_cost, 796: COUNT(*) as requests, 797: ROUND(SUM(cost_usd) / COUNT(*) * 1000, 4) as cost_per_request_mills 798: FROM routing_decisions 799: GROUP BY DATE(timestamp) 800: ORDER BY date DESC The above content does NOT show the entire file contents. Please note that any changes targeting the original code should remove the line number, colon, and leading space. 1: # WSL2 Automation & Installer Scripts 2: 3: > A comprehensive guide to automating WSL2 setup, service management, Docker/GPU configuration, and idempotent installer patterns for AI workstations. 4: 5: --- 6: 7: ## Table of Contents 8: 9: 1. [Creating Named WSL Distros](#creating-named-wsl-distros) 10: 2. [Managing WSL Instances](#managing-wsl-instances) 11: 3. [/etc/wsl.conf — Complete Reference](#wslconf-reference) 12: 4. [Installing Docker Engine (Without Docker Desktop)](#docker-engine) 13: 5. [NVIDIA Container Toolkit for GPU Passthrough](#nvidia-gpu) 14: 6. [systemd Services in WSL2](#systemd-services) 15: 7. [Creating systemd User Services](#user-services) 16: 8. [Idempotent Installer Pattern](#idempotent-installer) 17: 9. [Port Forwarding & Networking](#port-forwarding) 18: 10. [Gotchas & Lessons Learned](#gotchas) 19: 11. [Quick Reference](#quick-reference) 20: 21: --- 22: 23: ## 1. Creating Named WSL Distros 24: 25: WSL2 supports multiple named instances of the same distribution. This is essential for isolating environments (e.g., one for AI workloads, one for web dev). 26: 27: ### Install a Named Distro 28: 29: ```powershell 30: # List available distros 31: wsl --list --online 32: 33: # Install with a custom name 34: wsl --install Ubuntu-24.04 --name agentos 35: 36: # Or with Ubuntu 26.04 (if available) 37: wsl --install Ubuntu-26.04 --name agentos 38: ``` 39: 40: ### Full Setup Workflow 41: 42: ```powershell 43: # Step 1: Install the distro 44: wsl --install Ubuntu-24.04 --name agentos 45: 46: # Step 2: Set as d 42: | LiteLLM | 4000 | LLM proxy/router | 743: | n8n | 5678 | Workflow automation | 744: | Hermes Dashboard | 9119 | Agent monitoring | 745: | PostgreSQL | 5432 | Database | 746: | Redis | 6379 | Cache | 747: 748: ### Accessing WSL from Other Machines 749: 750: By default, WSL services are only accessible from the Windows host. To expose to the network: 751: 752: ```powershell 753: # PowerShell (Admin): Forward a port to the network 754: netsh interface portproxy add v4tov4 ` 755: listenport=5678 ` 756: listenaddress=0.0.0.0 ` 757: connectport=5678 ` 758: connectaddress=127.0.0.1 759: 760: # Don't forget Windows Firewall 761: New-NetFirewallRule ` 762: -DisplayName "n8n from network" ` 763: -Direction Inbound ` 764: -LocalPort 5678 ` 765: -Protocol TCP ` 766: -Action Allow 767: 768: # Remove the proxy when done 769: netsh interface portproxy delete v4tov4 ` 770: listenport=5678 ` 771: listenaddress=0.0.0.0 772: ``` 773: 774: --- 775: 776: ## 10. Gotchas & Lessons Learned 777: 778: ### 🚨 Gotcha #1: Unicode Encoding in WSL CLI Output 779: 780: **Problem:** When calling `wsl --list --verbose` from PowerShell, the output contains null bytes between characters (UTF-16LE encoding). This breaks string parsing: 781: 782: ```powershell 783: # This looks normal but is actually UTF-16LE: 784: $output = wsl --list --verbose 785: $output # Displays fine in terminal 786: $output.Length # Returns double the expected length! 787: ``` 788: 789: **The actual bytes:** 790: ``` 791: U 00 b 00 u 00 n 00 t 00 u 00 ← "Ubuntu" in UTF-16LE 792: ``` 793: 794: **Solution — Decode UTF-16LE:** 795: 796: ```powershell 797: # Method 1: Use [Console]::OutputEncoding 798: [Console]::OutputEncoding = [System.Text.Encoding]::Unicode 799: $output = wsl --list --verbose 800: The above content does NOT show the entire file contents. What is n8n? 24: 25: n8n is an open-source workflow automation tool — think Zapier but self-hosted, with a code-capable function node, and an AI agent node that connects to any OpenAI-compatible API. 26: 27: **Key advantages for developers:** 28: - **Self-hosted** — Your data stays on your infrastructure 29: - **Code nodes** — Write JavaScript/Python when visual nodes aren't enough 30: - **AI Agent node** — Connect to LiteLLM, Ollama, or any OpenAI-compatible endpoint 31: - **400+ integrations** — Gmail, Slack, Telegram, Google Sheets, GitHub, etc. 32: - **Webhook triggers** — Any external service can trigger a workflow 33: 34: --- 35: 36: ## 2. Self-Hosted Configuration 740: 741: ### SQLite (Default, Simple) 742: 743: ```bash 744: # Environment variables 745: DB_TYPE=sqlite 746: DB_SQLITE_DATABASE=/home/joel/.n8n/database.sqlite 747: 748: # Backup SQLite database 749: cp /home/joel/.n8n/database.sqlite /backups/n8n-$(date +%Y%m%d).sqlite 750: ``` 751: 752: ### PostgreSQL (Production, Recommended) 753: 754: ```bash 755: # Install PostgreSQL 756: sudo apt install -y postgresql postgresql-client 757: 758: # Create database and user 759: sudo -u postgres psql < "$BACKUP_DIR/workflows_$DATE.json" 794: 795: # Export credentials (encrypted, needs same encryption key to restore) 796: curl -s http://localhost:5678/api/v1/credentials \ 797: -H "X-N8N-API-KEY: your-api-key" \ 798: | jq '.' > "$BACKUP_DIR/credentials_$DATE.json" 799: 800: # Backup database The above content does NOT show the entire file contents. Please note that any changes targeting the original code should remove the line number, colon, and leading space. 1: # Writing Agent Handoff Documents 2: 3: > A comprehensive guide for AI agents and developers on creating effective handoff documents that enable seamless continuation of work across agent sessions, conversations, and team members. 4: 5: --- 6: 7: ## Table of Contents 8: 9: 1. [Why Handoffs Matter](#why-handoffs-matter) 10: 2. [The 3-Place Rule](#three-place-rule) 11: 3. [Document Structure — Complete Reference](#document-structure) 12: 4. [Complete Handoff Template](#complete-template) 13: 5. [Writing Each Section](#writing-each-section) 14: 6. [File Paths & Inventories](#file-inventories) 15: 7. [Subagent Prompt Documentation](#subagent-prompts) 16: 8. [Inline Config Schemas](#inline-configs) 17: 9. [Port Assignments & Service Maps](#port-assignments) 18: 10. [Credential Placeholders](#credential-placeholders) 19: 11. [Error Tracking](#error-tracking) 20: 12. [Conversation ID & Transcript Access](#conversation-ids) 21: 13. [Gotchas & Lessons Learned](#gotchas) 22: 14. [Pre-Handoff Verification Checklist](#verification-checklist) 23: 15. [Quick Reference](#quick-reference) 24: 25: --- 26: 27: ## 1. Why Handoffs Matter 28: 29: AI agents have **limited context windows** and **no persistent memory** between sessions. Without a handoff document: 30: 31: - The next agent starts from scratch, re-reading the entire codebase 32: - Design decisions are lost — the next agent might contradict them 33: - Partially completed work is abandoned or duplicated 34: - Time is wasted re-discovering project state 35: 36: A good handoff document is the Design decisions include rationale (not just "we chose X") 732: - [ ] Open questions are actionable (not vague) 733: ``` 734: 735: --- 736: 737: ## 15. Quick Reference 738: 739: ### Handoff Document Anatomy 740: 741: ``` 742: AGENT_HANDOFF.md 743: ├── Header (date, agent, conversation ID, status) 744: ├── User Profile (who, preferences) 745: ├── System Specs (OS, tools, versions) 746: ├── Project Status (one paragraph) 747: ├── File Inventory (path, size, status table) 748: ├── What's Done (checkbox list with verification) 749: ├── What's Pending (priority-ordered list) 750: ├── How to Continue (step-by-step) 751: ├── Architecture Reference (ASCII diagram) 752: ├── Port Assignments (table) 753: ├── Credentials Needed (names only!) 754: ├── Open Questions (decision points) 755: ├── Design Decisions (table with rationale) 756: ├── Error Log (what went wrong, why, fix) 757: └── Conversation ID (for transcript access) 758: ``` 759: 760: ### Size Targets 761: 762: | Document | Target Size | Max Size | 763: |----------|------------|----------| 764: | Full handoff | 10-12 KB | 15 KB | 765: | File inventory | 1-2 KB | 3 KB | 766: | Inline configs | 1-3 KB | 5 KB | 767: | Error log | 0.5-1 KB | 2 KB | 768: 769: ### Save Locations 770: 771: ``` 772: 1. /brain//AGENT_HANDOFF.md 773: 2. /AGENT_HANDOFF.md 774: 3. /AGENT_HANDOFF.md 775: ``` 776: 777: ### Verification Commands 778: 779: ```powershell 780: # Check all files exist 781: Get-ChildItem -Recurse \src\ -File | Select FullName, Length 782: 783: # Check project builds 784: cd && npm run build 785: 786: # Check handoff size 787: (Get-Item AGENT_HANDOFF.md).Length # Should be < 15360 bytes 788: ``` 789: 790: --- 791: 792: *Last updated: June 2026 | Author: Joel Thomas* 793: The above content shows the entire, complete file contents of the requested file.

19: ## 1. Agentic Frameworks
20: 
21: ### 1.1 LangGraph (LangChain)
22: 
23: | Attribute | Details |
24: |-----------|---------|
25: | **What** | Deterministic execution engine for stateful, multi-actor AI agent systems using graph-based state machines with cyclic logic support |
26: | **Version** | v1.2.5 (June 2026) |
27: | **Maturity** | Production-grade; industry standard. `AgentExecutor` deprecated in favor of LangGraph |
28: | **Key Features** | Checkpointers (PostgreSQL, MongoDB), Human-in-the-Loop interrupts, time-travel debugging, LangSmith integration for observability |
29: | **AgentOS Fit** | **HIGH** — Could replace ad-hoc skill orchestration with explicit state machines. The checkpointing system solves long-running workflow persistence. MCP integration is

80: ### Architecture Evolution Path
381: 
382: ```
383: Current:     Rules → Semantic → LLM → Model (via LiteLLM)
384:                                                     
385: Phase 1:     Rules → RouteLLM → LLM → Model (via OpenRouter)
386:              + MCP skills + Langfuse tracing + Semantic cache
387:                                                     
388: Phase 2:     Rules → RouteLLM → LLM → Model (via OpenRouter)
389:              + Mem0 memory + DSPy-optimized prompts
390:              + Circuit breaker + Guardrails middleware + Phi-4 local router
391:                                                     
392: Phase 3:     MCP-native skills with A2A discovery
393:              + LangGraph orchestration for complex workflows
394:              + Custom eval suite + annotation queue + Multi-agent delegation
395: ```

Simply closing the terminal doesn't restart the VM. 835: 836: **The correct workflow:** 837: ```powershell 838: # Step 1: Edit wsl.conf (from WSL) 839: wsl -d agentos -- sudo nano /etc/wsl.conf 840: 841: # Step 2: Shut down ALL WSL instances (from PowerShell) 842: wsl --shutdown 843: 844: # Step 3: Wait 3-5 seconds for full shutdown 845: Start-Sleep -Seconds 5 846: 847: # Step 4: "quick-reference"> 942: 943: ### Essential PowerShell Commands 944: 945: ```powershell 946: wsl --list --verbose # List all distros 947: wsl --shutdown # Restart WSL VM 948: wsl -d agentos # Enter a distro 949: wsl -d agentos -- # Run command in distro 950: wsl --export agentos file.tar # Backup 951: wsl --import name dir file.tar # Restore 952: wsl --terminate agentos # Stop one distro 953: wsl --unregister old-distro # Delete (destructive!) 954: ``` 955: 956: ### Essential WSL Bash Commands 957: 958: ```bash 959: systemctl status # Check service 960: journalctl -u -f # Follow logs 961: sudo systemctl daemon-reload # After .service file changes 962: sudo systemctl enable --now X # Enable + start 963: nvidia-smi # Check GPU 964: docker info # Check Docker 965: ``` 966: 967: ### Config File Locations 968: 969: | File | Location | Scope | 970: |------|----------|-------| 971: | `.wslconfig` | `%USERPROFILE%\.wslconfig` | All distros (Windows-side) | 972: | `wsl.conf` | `/etc/wsl.conf` | Per-distro (Linux-side) | 973: | Ollama service | `/etc/systemd/system/ollama.service` | System service | 974: | LiteLLM service | `/etc/systemd/system/litellm.service` | System service | 975: | n8n service | `/etc/systemd/system/n8n.service` | System service | 976: | Install markers | `~/.install-markers/` | Idempotent installer state | 977: 978: ### Port Map 979: 980: | Port | Service | Protocol | 981: |------|---------|----------| 982: | 4000 | LiteLLM Proxy | HTTP | 983: | 5432 | PostgreSQL | TCP | 984: | 5678 | n8n | HTTP | 985: | 6379 | Redis | TCP | 986: | 9119 | Hermes Dashboard | HTTP | 987: | 11434 | Ollama | HTTP | 988: 989: --- 990: 991: *Last updated: June 2026 | Author: Joel Thomas* 992: The above content does NOT show the entire file contents. # COMPREHENSIVE SUMMARY — Agent Skills Library (`d:\portfolio-draft\docs\skills\`)

## Overview
This is a **knowledge library** (last updated June 2026) built by/for **Joel Thomas** — a CS student at VIT Chennai (graduating 2027) — targeting Marquee placements (≥₹20 LPA). It serves dual audiences: **future AI agents** and **human developers**. The library covers 7 skill files plus a README index and an agentic AI research document, totaling ~240KB of documentation.

---

## 1. TECHNICAL PATTERNS

### Pattern: 3-Layer Cascade Router (model-routing.md, ~38KB)
The centerpiece technical pattern. Three classification layers with **decreasing speed and increasing intelligence**:
- **Layer 1 — Rules (0ms):** Regex pattern matching against categorized patterns (SIMPLE_PATTERNS, CODING_PATTERNS, REASONING_PATTERNS). Handles ~60% of traffic. Short messages (<20 chars) auto-classify as "simple."
- **Layer 2 — Semantic (5-20ms):** Embedding similarity using `semantic-router` library with `sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2` (80MB, local). Routes defined with 10-15 example utterances each.
- **Layer 3 — LLM Classifier (100-300ms):** Uses `groq/llama-3.3-8b-instruct` via LiteLLM for ambiguous requests. **Must always return a result, never None.** Default fallback tier is "coding" (mid-range, safest default).
- **Privacy Override (post-classification):** Regex detection of PII (SSN, Aadhaar, PAN, credit cards), credentials (API keys, passwords), and explicit keywords ("keep this local"). Forces local model when triggered.

Key pattern: `RouteDecision` dataclass with `model_tier`, `confidence`, `reason`, `privacy_override` fields.

### Pattern: Layered YAML Configuration (model-routing.md)
Configuration merges in three layers: `defaults.yaml → user config.yaml → environment variables`. Env vars use double-underscore for nesting (`ROUTER_MODEL__DEF
